title: Remove duplicates (sorted)
description: Remove duplicate values from a sorted linked list

examples.explanation: Start: head = 1. Outer: current = 1. Inner: runner = 1 (second node). Compare: 1 == 1, remove runner: current.next = runner.next (2). Runner becomes 2. Compare: 1 != 2, move runner to 3. Compare: 1 != 3, move runner to null. End inner. Move current to 2. Outer: current = 2. Inner: runner = 3. Compare: 2 != 3, move runner to 3 (second). Compare: 2 != 3, move runner to null. End inner. Move current to 3. Outer: current = 3. Inner: runner = 3 (second). Compare: 3 == 3, remove runner: current.next = runner.next (null). Runner becomes null. End inner. Move current to null. Done.

Start: head = 5. Outer: current = 5. Inner: runner = 5 (second). Compare: 5 == 5, remove runner: current.next = runner.next (third 5). Runner becomes 5 (third). Compare: 5 == 5, remove runner: current.next = runner.next (null). Runner becomes null. End inner. Move current to null. Done.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: For each node, traverse the rest of the list to find and remove duplicates by adjusting pointers. Use nested loops to compare each node with all subsequent nodes.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Remove duplicates from sorted linked list using brute force O(n²)
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of nested loop approach to remove duplicate nodes from a sorted linked list.
ladders.explanation: The brute force O(n²) approach solves the problem by using nested loops to compare each node with all subsequent nodes and remove duplicates. The intuition is that since the list is sorted, all duplicates of a value will appear consecutively, but this approach doesn't leverage that property—it treats it as a general duplicate removal problem. For each node (outer loop), we traverse all nodes after it (inner loop) and remove any nodes with the same value by adjusting pointers. The algorithm starts with a current pointer at the head. While current is not null, we set a runner pointer to current.next. While runner is not null, if runner.value equals current.value, we remove runner by setting current.next = runner.next (or updating previous runner pointer), then move runner to runner.next. If values differ, we move runner forward. After processing all nodes after current, we move current to current.next. Time complexity is O(n²) because for each of n nodes, we potentially traverse up to n nodes in the inner loop. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few pointers. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete removal logic that uses the key comparison and pointer adjustment subroutine. Common mistakes include not handling edge cases (empty list, single node), incorrectly updating pointers when removing nodes (losing reference to the rest of the list), and infinite loops when moving pointers. The real code pattern involves while loops with careful pointer updates.

examples.trace: Start: head = 1. Outer: current = 1. Inner: runner = 1 (second node). Compare: 1 == 1, remove runner: current.next = runner.next (2). Runner becomes 2. Compare: 1 != 2, move runner to 3. Compare: 1 != 3, move runner to null. End inner. Move current to 2. Outer: current = 2. Inner: runner = 3. Compare: 2 != 3, move runner to 3 (second). Compare: 2 != 3, move runner to null. End inner. Move current to 3. Outer: current = 3. Inner: runner = 3 (second). Compare: 3 == 3, remove runner: current.next = runner.next (null). Runner becomes null. End inner. Move current to null. Done.

Start: head = 5. Outer: current = 5. Inner: runner = 5 (second). Compare: 5 == 5, remove runner: current.next = runner.next (third 5). Runner becomes 5 (third). Compare: 5 == 5, remove runner: current.next = runner.next (null). Runner becomes null. End inner. Move current to null. Done.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compare and remove duplicate nodes in a linked list
ladders.desc: Implement the inner loop that finds and removes duplicates of a given node.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the brute force approach: given a current node, traverse the rest of the list and remove all nodes with the same value. The intuition is that to eliminate duplicates of a specific value, we need to scan forward and delete matching nodes. The algorithm takes a node 'current' as input. Initialize 'runner' to current.next and 'prevRunner' to current (to help with pointer adjustments). While runner is not null, if runner.value equals current.value, we remove runner by setting prevRunner.next = runner.next, then update runner = runner.next. If runner.value differs, update prevRunner = runner and runner = runner.next. This ensures we skip over duplicates. Time complexity for this sub-routine is O(n) in worst case (scanning entire list), but in context of full problem it's part of O(n²). Space is O(1). This connects to L0 as the inner loop logic, and to L2 as it relies on the comparison and pointer adjustment technique. Common mistakes include not updating prevRunner correctly when not removing (leading to missed nodes), or incorrectly linking after removal. The real code pattern uses a while loop with conditional pointer updates.

examples.trace: Start: current.value = 2, runner = 2 (second node), prevRunner = current (first 2). Step 1: runner.value = 2, equals current.value, remove: prevRunner.next = runner.next (3). Runner becomes 3. Step 2: runner.value = 3, not equal, update prevRunner = runner (3), runner = runner.next (2). Step 3: runner.value = 2, equals, remove: prevRunner.next = runner.next (4). Runner becomes 4. Step 4: runner.value = 4, not equal, update prevRunner = runner (4), runner = null. End.

Start: current.value = 5, runner = 5 (second), prevRunner = current. Step 1: runner.value = 5, equals, remove: prevRunner.next = runner.next (third 5). Runner becomes 5 (third). Step 2: runner.value = 5, equals, remove: prevRunner.next = runner.next (null). Runner becomes null. End.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Pointer manipulation to remove a node from linked list
ladders.desc: Learn how to delete a node by adjusting pointers without extra memory.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves removing a node from a singly linked list by updating the previous node's next pointer to skip the target node. The intuition is that in a linked list, nodes are connected via pointers; to remove a node, we need to bridge the gap between its predecessor and successor. Given a 'prev' node and a 'target' node (where target = prev.next), we remove target by setting prev.next = target.next. This effectively unlinks target from the list. If target is the head, we need special handling by updating head = head.next. The algorithm requires maintaining a reference to the previous node while traversing. Time complexity for removal is O(1) per node, but finding the node might take O(n). Space is O(1). This connects to L1 as the mechanism used in the duplicate removal subroutine, and to L3 as it builds on traversal skills. Common mistakes include losing reference to the list after removal (if not updating pointers correctly), or trying to remove a node without access to its predecessor. The real code pattern involves simple pointer assignment.

examples.input: List: A -> B -> C -> D, remove node C
examples.trace: Start: prev = B, target = C. Set prev.next = target.next (D). Now B points to D. C is removed. List becomes A -> B -> D.

examples.input: List: X -> Y -> Z, remove node X (head)
examples.trace: Start: head = X. Since target is head, set head = head.next (Y). Now head is Y. List becomes Y -> Z.

ladders.title: Building Block — Traverse a linked list with while loop
ladders.desc: Iterate through all nodes of a linked list using a pointer.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves traversing a linked list from head to tail using a while loop and a current pointer. The intuition is that linked lists are sequential structures where each node points to the next; traversal follows these pointers until null indicates the end. The algorithm initializes a pointer 'current' to the head. While current is not null, process current (e.g., print value), then set current = current.next to move to the next node. This continues until current becomes null. Time complexity is O(n) for n nodes. Space is O(1) for the pointer. This connects to L2 as traversal is needed to find nodes for removal, and to L4 as it uses node access operations. Common mistakes include infinite loops by not updating current, or off-by-one errors by stopping too early. The real code pattern is a simple while loop.

examples.trace: Start: current = 10. Step 1: process 10, current = 20. Step 2: process 20, current = 30. Step 3: process 30, current = null. End.
Start: current = 5. Step 1: process 5, current = null. End.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Access node value and next pointer in linked list
ladders.desc: Read data and pointer from a linked list node.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves accessing the value stored in a node and the pointer to the next node. In a linked list node structure (e.g., class Node with attributes 'value' and 'next'), given a node reference, we can get its value via node.value and its next node via node.next. The intuition is that nodes encapsulate data and linkage; accessing these allows navigation and manipulation. For example, if node = Node(5, next_node), then node.value returns 5 and node.next returns the reference to next_node. This is fundamental for all linked list operations. Time and space complexity are O(1). This connects to L3 as traversal relies on accessing node.next to move forward, and to L5 as it builds on the node concept. Common mistakes include accessing attributes on null nodes (causing errors) or misunderstanding that node.next is a reference, not the value. The real code pattern is direct attribute access.

examples.input: Node with value=3, next points to node with value=4
examples.trace: Given node A: A.value = 3, A.next = node B. Access A.value gives 3. Access A.next gives reference to B, which has B.value = 4.
Given node X: X.value = 9, X.next = null. Access X.value gives 9. Access X.next gives null, indicating end of list.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a linked list node?
ladders.desc: Understand the basic structure of a node in a singly linked list.
ladders.explanation: A linked list node is a fundamental data structure unit that stores two things: a value (or data) and a reference (or pointer) to the next node in the sequence. Think of it like a train car: the value is the cargo inside, and the reference is the coupling that connects to the next car. In code, this is often defined as a class or struct with attributes 'value' and 'next'. For example, in Python-style pseudocode: class Node: def __init__(self, value): self.value = value; self.next = None. The 'next' attribute points to another Node object or None if it's the last node. This structure allows dynamic memory allocation and efficient insertions/deletions compared to arrays. The intuition is that nodes link together to form a chain, enabling sequential access. Time and space for a single node are O(1). This connects to L4 as it defines the object we access. Common mistakes include confusing nodes with the list itself (a node is just one element), or not initializing 'next' properly. The real code pattern is a simple class definition.

examples.trace: Step 1: Define Node class. Step 2: Instantiate node = Node(10). This sets node.value = 10 and node.next = None by default.

examples.input: Create two nodes with values 5 and 8, link them
examples.trace: Step 1: Create node1 = Node(5). Step 2: Create node2 = Node(8). Step 3: Set node1.next = node2. Now node1.value=5, node1.next=node2; node2.value=8, node2.next=None.

approaches.name: Two Pointer O(n)
approaches.summary: Use two pointers: one to traverse the list and another to track the last unique node. Skip duplicates by adjusting pointers when consecutive nodes have the same value.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Remove Duplicates from Sorted Linked List Using Two Pointers
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of the two-pointer approach to remove duplicate values from a sorted linked list.
ladders.explanation: The two-pointer approach efficiently removes duplicates from a sorted linked list in O(n) time and O(1) space by maintaining two pointers: one to traverse the list and another to track the last unique node. The intuition is that since the list is sorted, all duplicate values will be consecutive. We can traverse the list once, and whenever we encounter a duplicate (current node value equals previous node value), we skip it by adjusting the next pointer of the last unique node. This approach preserves the original order of unique elements while removing duplicates in place. The algorithm starts with a dummy node to handle edge cases like empty lists or lists where all nodes are duplicates. We initialize two pointers: 'prev' pointing to the last unique node (starting at dummy), and 'curr' for traversal. As we traverse, if curr.val equals prev.val, we skip curr by setting prev.next = curr.next; otherwise, we move prev forward. The time complexity is O(n) as we traverse each node once, and space complexity is O(1) as we use only a few pointers. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete logic that uses the key subroutine of pointer adjustment. Common mistakes include not handling empty lists, forgetting to update pointers correctly when skipping duplicates, or not returning the modified list properly. Real code pattern: def removeDuplicates(head): dummy = ListNode(0, head); prev = dummy; curr = head; while curr: if prev.val == curr.val: prev.next = curr.next; else: prev = curr; curr = curr.next; return dummy.next.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize dummy(0) -> 1 -> 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 3, prev = dummy, curr = 1. Step 2: curr.val=1, prev.val=0 (different), move prev to curr (prev=1), curr to next (curr=1). Step 3: curr.val=1, prev.val=1 (equal), skip: set prev.next=curr.next (1->2), curr to next (curr=2). Step 4: curr.val=2, prev.val=1 (different), move prev to curr (prev=2), curr to next (curr=3). Step 5: curr.val=3, prev.val=2 (different), move prev to curr (prev=3), curr to next (curr=3). Step 6: curr.val=3, prev.val=3 (equal), skip: set prev.next=curr.next (3->None), curr to next (curr=None). Step 7: Return dummy.next = 1 -> 2 -> 3.

Step 1: Initialize dummy(0) -> 5 -> 5 -> 5, prev = dummy, curr = 5. Step 2: curr.val=5, prev.val=0 (different), move prev to curr (prev=5), curr to next (curr=5). Step 3: curr.val=5, prev.val=5 (equal), skip: set prev.next=curr.next (5->5), curr to next (curr=5). Step 4: curr.val=5, prev.val=5 (equal), skip: set prev.next=curr.next (5->None), curr to next (curr=None). Step 5: Return dummy.next = 5.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Adjusting Pointers to Skip Duplicate Nodes
ladders.desc: Implement the core pointer adjustment logic that skips duplicate nodes in a sorted linked list.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on the essential operation of adjusting pointers to skip duplicate nodes when traversing a sorted linked list. The intuition is that in a sorted list, duplicates appear consecutively, so we can detect them by comparing adjacent nodes. When we find a duplicate (current node value equals the previous node's value), we skip the current node by making the previous node's next pointer point to the current node's next node, effectively removing the duplicate from the chain. This operation maintains the list structure while eliminating duplicates. The algorithm requires two pointers: 'prev' (pointing to the last unique node) and 'curr' (pointing to the current node being examined). We traverse the list, and at each step: if prev.val == curr.val, we set prev.next = curr.next to skip curr; otherwise, we move prev to curr to mark it as the new last unique node. Then we advance curr to the next node. Time complexity is O(n) for traversal, and space is O(1). This feeds into L0 by being the critical step in the full solution. Common mistakes include not updating pointers in the correct order (e.g., moving prev before checking), or mishandling the case when prev is initially a dummy node. Real code pattern: while curr: if prev.val == curr.val: prev.next = curr.next; else: prev = curr; curr = curr.next.

examples.input: prev points to node 2, curr points to node 2 (duplicate), list: 1 -> 2 -> 2 -> 3
examples.trace: Step 1: prev.val=2, curr.val=2 (equal). Step 2: Skip curr by setting prev.next = curr.next (2->3). Step 3: Move curr to curr.next (now curr points to 3). Step 4: Result: prev still points to 2, list is 1 -> 2 -> 3.

examples.input: prev points to node 5, curr points to node 6 (different), list: 5 -> 6 -> 6
examples.trace: Step 1: prev.val=5, curr.val=6 (different). Step 2: Move prev to curr (prev now points to 6). Step 3: Move curr to curr.next (curr now points to 6). Step 4: Now prev.val=6, curr.val=6 (equal), will skip in next step if continued.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Detecting Consecutive Duplicates in Sorted Linked List
ladders.desc: Identify when consecutive nodes have the same value in a sorted linked list.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the central insight of detecting consecutive duplicate values in a sorted linked list, which is fundamental to the two-pointer approach. The intuition is that because the list is sorted, any duplicate values must appear next to each other—there's no way for duplicates to be separated by different values. Therefore, we can detect duplicates simply by comparing each node with its immediate predecessor as we traverse the list. This comparison is efficient and requires only O(1) space for storing the previous node's value. The algorithm involves traversing the list with a pointer 'curr', while keeping track of the previous node's value (initially set to a sentinel value like None or using a dummy node). At each step, we compare curr.val with the previous value: if they're equal, we've found a duplicate; if not, we update the previous value to curr.val and continue. This detection logic is what enables us to know when to skip nodes in the full solution. Time complexity is O(n) for the traversal, space is O(1). This connects to L1 by providing the condition for pointer adjustment. Common mistakes include not initializing the previous value correctly, or comparing nodes that aren't consecutive. Real code pattern: prev_val = None; while curr: if prev_val == curr.val: duplicate detected; else: prev_val = curr.val; curr = curr.next.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize prev_val = None, curr points to 1. Step 2: curr.val=1, prev_val=None (different), set prev_val=1, move curr to next (curr=1). Step 3: curr.val=1, prev_val=1 (equal), detect duplicate at current node, move curr to next (curr=2). Step 4: curr.val=2, prev_val=1 (different), set prev_val=2, move curr to next (curr=3). Step 5: curr.val=3, prev_val=2 (different), set prev_val=3, move curr to next (curr=None).

Step 1: prev_val = None, curr points to 5. Step 2: curr.val=5, prev_val=None (different), set prev_val=5, move curr to next (curr=6). Step 3: curr.val=6, prev_val=5 (different), set prev_val=6, move curr to next (curr=6). Step 4: curr.val=6, prev_val=6 (equal), detect duplicate at current node, move curr to next (curr=7). Step 5: curr.val=7, prev_val=6 (different), set prev_val=7, move curr to next (curr=None).

testCases.expected: Duplicates at positions: node 2 (value 1), node 4 (value 2), node 6 (value 3)
Duplicates at positions: node 2 (value 5), node 3 (value 5), node 4 (value 5)

ladders.title: Building Block — Traversing a Linked List with Two Pointers
ladders.desc: Practice traversing a linked list while maintaining two pointers: one for current position and one for previous node.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves traversing a linked list using two pointers, which is essential for the two-pointer approach to remove duplicates. The intuition is that by keeping a 'prev' pointer to the previous node and a 'curr' pointer to the current node, we can compare adjacent nodes and perform operations based on their relationship. This technique allows us to look back at the previous node while processing the current one, enabling detection of duplicates and pointer adjustments. The algorithm starts with prev initialized to a dummy node or None, and curr to the head of the list. We traverse by moving both pointers forward: typically, we update prev to curr, then curr to curr.next, ensuring prev always lags one step behind curr. This traversal pattern is common in linked list problems for in-place modifications. Time complexity is O(n) for the traversal, space is O(1). This feeds into L2 by providing the mechanism to detect consecutive duplicates. Common mistakes include not updating pointers in sync (e.g., moving curr before prev), or losing reference to the list head. Real code pattern: prev = dummy; curr = head; while curr: # process curr; prev = curr; curr = curr.next.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize prev = dummy(0), curr = 1. Step 2: Process curr=1, set prev = curr (prev=1), curr = curr.next (curr=2). Step 3: Process curr=2, set prev = curr (prev=2), curr = curr.next (curr=3). Step 4: Process curr=3, set prev = curr (prev=3), curr = curr.next (curr=None).
Step 1: prev = dummy(0), curr = 4. Step 2: Process curr=4, prev=4, curr=5. Step 3: Process curr=5, prev=5, curr=6. Step 4: Process curr=6, prev=6, curr=7. Step 5: Process curr=7, prev=7, curr=None.

testCases.input: List: 10 -> 20 -> 30, traverse with prev and curr
List: 5 -> 5 -> 6, traverse with prev and curr
List: 1 -> 1 -> 1, traverse with prev and curr
List: 2 -> 3 -> 4 -> 5, traverse with prev and curr
testCases.expected: Steps: prev=0,curr=2; prev=2,curr=3; prev=3,curr=4; prev=4,curr=5; prev=5,curr=None

testCases.input: List: 8 -> 9 -> 10, traverse with prev and curr
List: 0 -> 0 -> 1 -> 1, traverse with prev and curr
testCases.expected: Steps: prev=0,curr=0; prev=0,curr=0; prev=0,curr=1; prev=1,curr=1; prev=1,curr=None

testCases.input: List: 6 -> 7 -> 8 -> 9, traverse with prev and curr
testCases.expected: Steps: prev=0,curr=6; prev=6,curr=7; prev=7,curr=8; prev=8,curr=9; prev=9,curr=None

testCases.input: List: 3 -> 3 -> 4 -> 4, traverse with prev and curr
testCases.expected: Steps: prev=0,curr=3; prev=3,curr=3; prev=3,curr=4; prev=4,curr=4; prev=4,curr=None

testCases.input: List: 1 -> 2 -> 2 -> 3, traverse with prev and curr
testCases.expected: Steps: prev=0,curr=1; prev=1,curr=2; prev=2,curr=2; prev=2,curr=3; prev=3,curr=None

testCases.input: List: 9 -> 10 -> 11, traverse with prev and curr

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Node Values in Linked List
ladders.desc: Compare the values of two nodes in a linked list to check for equality.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves comparing the values stored in two nodes of a linked list, which is crucial for detecting duplicates in the two-pointer approach. The intuition is simple: since we want to remove duplicates, we need to identify when two nodes have the same value. In a linked list, each node typically has a 'val' attribute (the data) and a 'next' pointer (reference to the next node). To compare two nodes, we access their 'val' attributes and check if they are equal using the equality operator (==). This operation is O(1) in time and space, as it involves just reading two values and performing a comparison. It's a building block for more complex logic like detecting consecutive duplicates. In the context of the problem, we compare the current node's value with the previous node's value to see if they're the same. This feeds into L3 by being part of the traversal logic. Common mistakes include comparing node objects directly (which compares references, not values), or not handling cases where nodes might be None. Real code pattern: if node1.val == node2.val: # values are equal.

examples.input: Node A: val=5, Node B: val=5
examples.trace: Step 1: Access nodeA.val = 5. Step 2: Access nodeB.val = 5. Step 3: Compare 5 == 5, result is True.

examples.input: Node X: val=3, Node Y: val=7
examples.trace: Step 1: Access nodeX.val = 3. Step 2: Access nodeY.val = 7. Step 3: Compare 3 == 7, result is False.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Linked List Node Structure
ladders.desc: Learn the basic structure of a node in a linked list, including value and next pointer.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding what a node is in a linked list. A linked list is a linear data structure where each element, called a node, contains two parts: a 'value' (or 'data') that stores the actual data, and a 'next' pointer that references the next node in the sequence. The intuition is that nodes are connected in a chain via these pointers, allowing dynamic memory allocation and efficient insertions/deletions compared to arrays. In many programming languages, a node is defined as a class or structure with attributes for value and next. For example, in Python-style pseudocode: class ListNode: def __init__(self, val=0, next=None): self.val = val; self.next = next. The 'val' can be any data type (e.g., integer), and 'next' is either a reference to another ListNode or None (indicating the end of the list). This foundation is crucial because all operations on linked lists, including traversing and comparing nodes, rely on accessing these attributes. Time and space analysis isn't directly applicable here, but accessing node attributes is O(1). This feeds into L4 by providing the objects we compare. Common mistakes include confusing nodes with their values, or not understanding that 'next' is a pointer/reference. Real code pattern: node = ListNode(5) creates a node with val=5, next=None.

examples.trace: Step 1: Define ListNode class. Step 2: Instantiate node = ListNode(3). Step 3: node.val is set to 3. Step 4: node.next is set to None by default.

examples.input: Create two nodes: first with value 1, second with value 2, linked together
examples.trace: Step 1: Create node1 = ListNode(1). Step 2: Create node2 = ListNode(2). Step 3: Set node1.next = node2. Step 4: Now node1.val=1, node1.next=node2; node2.val=2, node2.next=None.

testCases.input: Node with value 10, next points to another node with value 20
testCases.expected: val=10, next points to node with val=20

testCases.input: Node with value 7, next is None
Node with value 3, next points to node with value 4
testCases.expected: val=3, next points to node with val=4

testCases.expected: val=2, next points to node with val=2

testCases.input: Node with value 6, next points to None
Node with value 8, next points to node with value 9
testCases.expected: val=8, next points to node with val=9

testCases.input: Node with value 1, next points to node with value 1
testCases.expected: val=1, next points to node with val=1

approaches.name: Hash Set O(n)
approaches.summary: Traverse the list while storing seen values in a hash set. Remove nodes if their value is already in the set, adjusting pointers accordingly.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Remove Duplicates from Sorted Linked List Using Hash Set
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of removing duplicate values from a sorted linked list using a hash set approach.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution to remove duplicate values from a sorted linked list using a hash set. The intuition is that since we're dealing with a sorted list, duplicates will appear consecutively, but we can use a hash set to track seen values regardless of order. We traverse the list once, maintaining a hash set of values we've encountered. For each node, if its value is already in the set, we remove it by adjusting the previous node's next pointer to skip it. If it's not in the set, we add it to the set and move forward. The algorithm starts with a dummy node to handle edge cases like removing the head. We maintain two pointers: prev (initially pointing to dummy) and curr (starting at head). We iterate while curr exists. If curr.val is in seen set, we skip curr by setting prev.next = curr.next, then move curr = curr.next. Otherwise, we add curr.val to seen, set prev = curr, and move curr = curr.next. Finally, return dummy.next as the new head. Time complexity is O(n) where n is number of nodes, as we traverse once and hash set operations are O(1) average. Space complexity is O(n) for the hash set storing unique values. This connects to L1 as it uses the key subroutine of node removal with pointer adjustment. Common mistakes include not handling empty lists, forgetting to update prev pointer when not removing, and memory leaks in languages requiring manual deallocation. Real code pattern: def removeDuplicates(head): if not head: return None; dummy = ListNode(0, head); prev = dummy; curr = head; seen = set(); while curr: if curr.val in seen: prev.next = curr.next; else: seen.add(curr.val); prev = curr; curr = curr.next; return dummy.next.

examples.trace: Start: head = 1, dummy = 0->1, prev = dummy, curr = 1, seen = {}. Step 1: curr.val = 1 not in seen, add 1 to seen = {1}, prev = node 1, curr = next node 1. Step 2: curr.val = 1 in seen, remove: prev.next = curr.next (node 1 points to node 2), curr = node 2. Step 3: curr.val = 2 not in seen, add 2 to seen = {1,2}, prev = node 2, curr = node 3. Step 4: curr.val = 3 not in seen, add 3 to seen = {1,2,3}, prev = node 3, curr = node 3. Step 5: curr.val = 3 in seen, remove: prev.next = curr.next (node 3 points to None), curr = None. End: return dummy.next = node 1, list = 1->2->3.

Start: head = 5, dummy = 0->5, prev = dummy, curr = 5, seen = {}. Step 1: curr.val = 5 not in seen, add 5 to seen = {5}, prev = node 5, curr = next node 5. Step 2: curr.val = 5 in seen, remove: prev.next = curr.next (node 5 points to node 5), curr = node 5. Step 3: curr.val = 5 in seen, remove: prev.next = curr.next (node 5 points to None), curr = None. End: return dummy.next = node 5, list = 5.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Remove Node from Linked List with Pointer Adjustment
ladders.desc: Given a linked list, remove a specific node by adjusting the previous node's next pointer.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the most important helper function for the full problem: removing a node from a singly linked list when you have access to the previous node. The intuition is that in a singly linked list, you cannot directly remove a node without knowing its predecessor because you need to update the next pointer of the node before it to skip over the node being removed. We're given prev (the node before the one to remove) and curr (the node to remove). The algorithm is simple: set prev.next = curr.next. This effectively bypasses curr, removing it from the list. If curr has a next node, prev now points to it; if curr is the last node, prev.next becomes None. We then typically move curr to curr.next to continue traversal. Time complexity is O(1) since it's a constant-time pointer update. Space complexity is O(1) as no extra data structures are used. This feeds into L0 as it's the core operation performed whenever we find a duplicate value in the hash set. Common mistakes include trying to remove a node without a reference to prev (which requires traversal from head), not handling edge cases like removing the head (which is why L0 uses a dummy node), and forgetting to update traversal pointers after removal. Real code pattern: def removeNode(prev, curr): prev.next = curr.next. In context: if duplicate found, do this then curr = curr.next. Connection to L0: This is executed in the while loop when curr.val is in seen set.

examples.trace: Start: list = 1->2->3, prev points to node 1, curr points to node 2. Operation: prev.next = curr.next. Since curr.next is node 3, set prev.next (node 1's next) to node 3. Result: node 1 now points to node 3, node 2 is removed. List becomes 1->3.

Start: list = 5->6->7, prev points to node 6, curr points to node 7. Operation: prev.next = curr.next. Since curr.next is None, set prev.next (node 6's next) to None. Result: node 6 now points to None, node 7 is removed. List becomes 5->6.

testCases.input: List: A -> B -> C, prev = A, curr = B
List: X -> Y -> Z, prev = Y, curr = Z
List: a -> b -> c -> d, prev = c, curr = d
List: p -> q -> r, prev = p, curr = q
List: x -> y -> z -> w, prev = z, curr = w

ladders.title: Core Logic — Tracking Seen Values with Hash Set During Traversal
ladders.desc: Maintain a hash set of encountered values while iterating through a linked list to identify duplicates.
ladders.explanation: This is the central insight of the hash set approach: using a hash set to keep track of values we've already seen during traversal to detect duplicates. The intuition is that a hash set provides O(1) average-time lookups and insertions, allowing us to quickly check if a value has appeared before. As we traverse the list node by node, for each node's value, we check if it exists in the set. If it does, it's a duplicate; if not, it's unique so far, and we add it to the set. This works regardless of whether the list is sorted or not, though the problem specifies sorted. The algorithm involves initializing an empty hash set, then traversing the list with a pointer. At each node: if node.val in set, mark as duplicate; else, add node.val to set. Time complexity is O(n) for traversal with O(1) hash operations per node. Space complexity is O(k) where k is number of unique values, up to O(n). This feeds into L1 as it determines when to call the removal subroutine: when a duplicate is detected via the hash set. Common mistakes include not initializing the set, confusing the set with the list structure, or trying to store nodes instead of values. Real code pattern: seen = set(); curr = head; while curr: if curr.val in seen: # duplicate; else: seen.add(curr.val); curr = curr.next. Connection to L0: This logic is embedded in the main loop to decide whether to remove a node.

examples.trace: Start: seen = {}, curr points to node 1 (value 1). Step 1: curr.val = 1 not in seen, add to seen = {1}, move curr to node 2. Step 2: curr.val = 2 not in seen, add to seen = {1,2}, move curr to node 1. Step 3: curr.val = 1 in seen, duplicate detected, move curr to node 3. Step 4: curr.val = 3 not in seen, add to seen = {1,2,3}, move curr to None. End: seen = {1,2,3}, duplicate found at step 3.

Start: seen = {}, curr points to node 5 (value 5). Step 1: curr.val = 5 not in seen, add to seen = {5}, move curr to node 5. Step 2: curr.val = 5 in seen, duplicate detected, move curr to node 5. Step 3: curr.val = 5 in seen, duplicate detected, move curr to None. End: seen = {5}, duplicates at steps 2 and 3.

testCases.expected: Set: {1}, duplicates at second and third nodes
Set: {0}, duplicates at second, third, fourth nodes

ladders.title: Building Block — Traversing a Singly Linked List with Pointer Updates
ladders.desc: Iterate through a linked list using a current pointer, updating it to move to the next node.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is essential for the hash set approach: traversing a singly linked list by maintaining a pointer that moves from node to node. The intuition is that a linked list is a sequential data structure where each node points to the next, so traversal requires following these pointers. We start with a pointer (often called curr) initialized to the head of the list. While curr is not None (i.e., we haven't reached the end), we process the current node (e.g., check its value), then update curr = curr.next to move to the next node. This continues until curr becomes None, indicating the end of the list. The algorithm is: curr = head; while curr != None: # process curr; curr = curr.next. Time complexity is O(n) for n nodes, as we visit each once. Space complexity is O(1) for the pointer. This feeds into L2 as it provides the mechanism to iterate through the list while checking values against the hash set. Common mistakes include infinite loops by not updating curr, off-by-one errors by stopping too early or late, and losing the head reference if not saved separately. Real code pattern: def traverse(head): curr = head; while curr: print(curr.val); curr = curr.next. Connection to L0: This traversal is the outer loop in the full solution.

examples.trace: Start: curr = head = node 1. Step 1: curr is not None, print curr.val = 1, update curr = curr.next = node 2. Step 2: curr is not None, print curr.val = 2, update curr = curr.next = node 3. Step 3: curr is not None, print curr.val = 3, update curr = curr.next = None. Step 4: curr is None, loop ends.

Start: curr = head = node 5. Step 1: curr is not None, print curr.val = 5, update curr = curr.next = node 6. Step 2: curr is not None, print curr.val = 6, update curr = curr.next = None. Step 3: curr is None, loop ends.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Node Value and Next Pointer in Linked List
ladders.desc: Retrieve the value stored in a linked list node and the reference to its next node.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves interacting with a single node in a linked list: accessing its value and its next pointer. In a typical singly linked list implementation, each node has two attributes: val (or data) storing the value, and next storing a reference to the next node in the list. The intuition is that nodes are objects containing data and a link to the successor. To access the value, use node.val; to access the next node, use node.next. If node.next is None, it indicates the end of the list. This operation is basic because it's the primary way to read information from the list without modifying it. The algorithm is straightforward: given a node, read its val and next attributes. Time and space complexity are O(1) as it's a direct attribute access. This feeds into L3 as it's used during traversal to get the current node's value for processing and to move to the next node. Common mistakes include trying to access val or next on a None node (which causes errors), confusing val with the node object itself, or misunderstanding that next is a pointer/reference, not the value. Real code pattern: In Python, if node is not None: value = node.val; next_node = node.next. Connection to L0: This is used repeatedly in the loop to check curr.val and update curr = curr.next.

examples.input: Node with val=5, next pointing to another node with val=10
examples.trace: Given a node object: node.val returns 5. node.next returns a reference to the next node. If we access node.next.val, it would be 10. This shows how we can chain accesses to traverse.
Given a node object: node.val returns 3. node.next returns None, indicating this is the last node in the list. Attempting node.next.val would cause an error because None has no val attribute.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding a Linked List Node Structure
ladders.desc: Define what a node is in a linked list: an object containing data and a reference to the next node.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding the basic building block of a linked list—the node. A node is a fundamental data structure element that typically consists of two parts: 1) Data (or value): This holds the actual information, such as an integer, string, or object. 2) Next (or link): This is a reference or pointer to the next node in the sequence. In a singly linked list, each node points only to the next node; the last node points to None (or null) to indicate the end. The intuition is that nodes are like chain links: each link holds something and connects to the next, forming a sequence. This is foundational because without nodes, there is no linked list. In code, a node is often defined as a class with attributes for val and next. For example, in Python: class ListNode: def __init__(self, val=0, next=None): self.val = val; self.next = next. Time and space analysis isn't applicable here as it's a definition, but creating a node is O(1) time and space. This feeds into L4 as it provides the structure whose attributes we access. Common mistakes include confusing nodes with arrays, thinking nodes store indices, or not understanding that next is a reference (not a copy). Real code pattern: node = ListNode(5) creates a node with val=5, next=None. Connection to L0: The entire linked list in the problem is composed of such nodes.

examples.trace: We instantiate a ListNode object: node = ListNode(10). This calls __init__ with val=10, next=None. The node now has attribute node.val = 10 and node.next = None. It's a standalone node not connected to others.

examples.input: Create two nodes: first with value 1, second with value 2, link first to second
examples.trace: Step 1: Create second node: node2 = ListNode(2). Step 2: Create first node linked to second: node1 = ListNode(1, node2). Now node1.val = 1, node1.next = node2. node2.val = 2, node2.next = None. This forms a mini-list: node1 -> node2.

testCases.input: Node with val=3 and next pointing to another node
Node with val=9 linked to node with val=10
Node with val=4 and next pointing to node with val=5
Node with val=8 linked to None
Node with val=1 and next pointing to node with val=2
